questions.q: How does a pod specify which scheduler should handle its placement?
options: Via a label on the pod: `scheduler: my-custom-scheduler`
Via the `schedulerName` field in the pod spec
Via a ConfigMap referenced in the pod spec
Via a Node Affinity rule targeting the scheduler node
questions.explanation: The schedulerName field in the pod spec tells Kubernetes which scheduler to use. If omitted, it defaults to 'default-scheduler'.

questions.q: What happens if `leaderElect: false` is set in the scheduler config and you scale the scheduler Deployment to 2 replicas?
options: Both replicas share work by splitting pods alphabetically
Both replicas may schedule the same pod simultaneously, causing conflicts
The second replica automatically becomes the leader
Kubernetes prevents scaling beyond 1 when leaderElect is false
questions.explanation: With leaderElect: false, there is no leader election and multiple replicas will all try to schedule pods, potentially causing race conditions. Use leaderElect: true (or keep replicas: 1) for production.

questions.q: What is the benefit of Scheduler Profiles compared to running multiple separate scheduler processes?
options: Multiple profiles run in a single binary, reducing operational overhead
Profiles allow different schedulers for different namespaces
Profiles are faster because they skip the Filter phase
questions.explanation: Scheduler Profiles allow you to define multiple named scheduling behaviors in a single KubeSchedulerConfiguration. Pods reference different profiles via schedulerName without needing separate deployments.

questions.q: Which extension point runs AFTER Filter and handles the case where NO node passed filtering?
questions.explanation: PostFilter runs when the Filter phase eliminates all nodes. The DefaultPreemption plugin runs here and attempts to preempt (evict) lower-priority pods to free up a node.
